user_request: Draft a suit for recovery of Rs.20,00,000/- paid as advance for a business transaction which failed due to Defendant's default. Plead total failure of consideration under Section 65 of Indian Contract Act. Claim refund with interest and costs. Include cause of action paragraph with accrual date, continuing nature of cause of action, valuation, court fee, and proper verification clause. Draft suitable for filing before the District Court.

facts.summary: Plaintiff seeks recovery of Rs. 20,00,000 paid as advance for a business transaction that failed due to the Defendant's default. The suit alleges total failure of consideration under Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act and claims a refund with interest and costs.

slots.value: Recovery of advance with interest and costs

missing_fields: Date of failure of transaction/cause of action
assumptions: Jurisdiction lies with the District Court based on valuation or cause of action
Suit is within the limitation period

the valuation for jurisdiction in certain suits is the same. Under s 7 cl (iv)(f) of the Court Fees Act, 1870, the 
valuation for purposes of court fees in a suit for accounts lies in the discretion of the plaintiff. Therefore, in a suit 
for accounts, the plaintiffs valuation in the plaint fixes the jurisdiction of the court. 2 In a suit for unsettled 
accounts, fixed court fee is payable and for jurisdictional purposes, such a suit can be valued in terms of 
valuation as fixed by the plaintiff. 3 In a suit for partition, the value for the purpose of jurisdiction is the value of 
the share claimed by the plaintiff and not the value of the entire estate in which the partition is sought. 4 In a suit 
for recovery of property from the tenant whose tenancy has been terminated, the valuation of suit for 
jurisdictional purposes should be on the basis of annual rent or profit. Suit so valued along with the claim of 
damage exceeding pecuniary jurisdiction of the court is liable to be returned for prosecution before the proper 
court.5

3. Alternative and inconsistent allegations. The rule now under consideration does not prohibit inconsistent 
pleadings. A person may rely upon one set of facts, if he can succeed in proving them, and he may rely upon 
another set of facts if he can succeed in proving them; and it appears to me to be far too strict a construction of this 
Order to say that he must make up his mind on which particular line he will put his case, when perhaps he is very 
much in the dark.52 The rules as to joinder of defendants (O 1, r 3) and causes of action (O 2, r 3), as also the rules 
as to the reliefs that may be claimed by a plaintiff (O 7, rr 78) clearly show that either party may allege in his 
pleadings two or more inconsistent sets of material facts and claim relief thereunder in the alternative. 53 A plaintiff 
may rely upon several different rights...alternatively, although they may be inconsistent. 54 But then as to each of 
those he ought to set out the facts upon which he would have to rely as facts to maintain that right. 55 So, too, a 
defendant may, by his written statement, raise as many distinct and separate, and, therefore, inconsistent, defences 
as he may think proper. 56 But this is subject to the provision contained in r 16 below which provides that the court

sections.body:
SUIT FOR RECOVERY OF Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- (Rupees {{CLAIM_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only) WITH INTEREST AND COSTS
(Filed under Section 26 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908)

sections.heading: BETWEEN
sections.instruction: Draft the parties section listing plaintiff and defendant(s) with full particulars: name, age, occupation, and address. Use sui juris averment for competency. If any detail is missing, use the appropriate {{PLACEHOLDER}}.

sections.heading: JURISDICTION
sections.instruction: Draft the jurisdiction section establishing territorial and pecuniary jurisdiction. For territorial jurisdiction, state SPECIFIC facts: where the Defendant resides or carries on business, where payment was made, where the agreement was executed, or where the cause of action arose — with concrete place references. For pecuniary jurisdiction, state the claim amount and that it falls within this court's monetary limits.

sections.heading: FACTS OF THE CASE
sections.instruction: Draft the facts section as numbered paragraphs in chronological narrative form. Each paragraph must tell a story — not state conclusions. Cover: (1) How the parties came to know each other and the nature of their relationship, (2) What was agreed — the specific terms of the transaction/agreement, (3) What obligations each party undertook and the timeline for performance, (4) How payment was made — specific amount, mode, date, (5) What exactly constituted the defendant's failure — describe the specific default with detail, (6) What happened after the default — communications, demands, legal notice and response. Reference documents as 'Annexure [LABEL]'. Do NOT state legal conclusions in this section — keep it purely factual.

sections.heading: LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS
sections.instruction: Draft the legal basis section in paragraph form. For each statutory provision: (1) State what the provision says — the legal rule it creates, (2) Explain what factual conditions trigger its application, (3) Show how the facts of this case satisfy those conditions. If applicable, include an alternative plea (e.g., Section 73 ICA as alternative to Section 65 ICA) using 'In the alternative and without prejudice to the above...' framing. Include defensive pleading: pre-emptively neutralize likely defense arguments (e.g., no part performance was rendered, no forfeiture clause exists, no adjustment made, no counter-claim exists). Cite ONLY provisions from the verified list.

must_include.description: At least one statutory section cited
Legal consequence explained (not just section named)

sections.heading: CAUSE OF ACTION
sections.instruction: Draft the cause of action section in PARAGRAPH prose (not numbered sub-headings). Must cover: (1) ORIGIN — when and how the right to sue first arose, anchored to the default/failure date, (2) FURTHER ACCRUAL — when the cause of action further arose (e.g., failure to comply with legal notice), (3) CONTINUITY — that the cause of action is continuing because the defendant continues to wrongfully retain the money. The cause must be anchored to the failure/default date, NOT the notice date. Write in flowing paragraph form — avoid mechanical sub-headings like '(a) underlying obligation'.

sections.heading: LIMITATION
sections.instruction: Draft the limitation section stating: (1) the applicable Article of the Limitation Act, 1963 Schedule (from enrichment or use placeholder), (2) the limitation period, (3) the starting date of limitation (must be anchored to cause_of_action_date/default_date, NOT notice_date), (4) that the suit is within time. If limitation article is not available in enrichment, use {{LIMITATION_ARTICLE}} placeholder.

sections.heading: VALUATION AND COURT FEE
sections.instruction: Draft the valuation and court fee section stating: (1) suit valuation equals the claim amount, (2) court fee calculated per applicable state court fees act. Use court_fee context from web search if available, otherwise use {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}} placeholder.

sections.heading: INTEREST
sections.instruction: Draft the interest section with a 3-part structure: (1) Pre-suit interest — from default date to filing date with rate and justification, (2) Pendente lite interest — during pendency of suit under Section 34 CPC with rate, (3) Future interest — post-decree until realization with rate. Justify the interest rate: state why the claimed rate is appropriate (commercial transaction, wrongful retention, prevailing bank rate, contractual understanding). Cite Section 34 of CPC for pendente lite interest.

sections.heading: PRAYER
sections.instruction: Draft the prayer section with at least 6 specific sub-prayers as lettered items (a, b, c, ...). Each prayer must state exact amounts or rate placeholders — never vague. Must include: (a) decree for principal amount with Rs. figure, (b) pre-suit interest with rate and date range, (c) pendente lite interest under Section 34 CPC with rate, (d) future interest post-decree with rate until realization, (e) costs of the suit including pre-litigation costs for sending legal notice, (f) any other relief the court deems fit and proper in the facts and circumstances.

sections.heading: LIST OF DOCUMENTS RELIED UPON
sections.instruction: Generate a numbered list of documents the plaintiff relies upon, mapping to the annexure scheme. For each document: Serial No., Description, Annexure Label. Only list documents mentioned in evidence. If evidence is empty, list standard expected documents with {{DOCUMENT_DESCRIPTION}} placeholders.

sections.heading: VERIFICATION
sections.body:
I, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, the Plaintiff above-named, do hereby verify that the contents of the above plaint are true and correct to my knowledge, and the contents based on legal advice are believed to be true on the basis of such advice. No part of this plaint is false and nothing material has been concealed therefrom.

Verified at {{COURT_PLACE}} on this {{FILING_DATE}}.

As per Order VI Rule 15 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908.

filled_sections.heading: JURISDICTION
filled_sections.text:
The territorial jurisdiction of this Court is proper in the District Court at {{COURT_PLACE}}, {{STATE}}, as the Defendant resides and carries on business at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}, which lies within the local limits of this Court's territorial jurisdiction. The advance payment of Rs 20,00,000 was transferred from the Plaintiff's account held at {{BANK_NAME}} {{BANK_BRANCH}} on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}}, a banking source situated within the local limits of this Court's jurisdiction. The agreement between the parties was executed on {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} at {{AGREEMENT_PLACE}}, which is also within the territorial reach of this Court. The cause of action first arose on {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}} in relation to the contract, and the Defendant defaulted on {{DEFAULT_DATE}} by failing to perform and to repay the sum due. This Court is invoked for pecuniary purposes because the claim amount is Rs 20,00,000, which falls within the monetary limits of the District Court for suits of this nature; the pecuniary limit for this Court is Rs {{PECUNIARY_LIMIT}}. The Place for purposes of this section is {{COURT_PLACE}}.
---SECTION_TEXT---

filled_sections.heading: FACTS OF THE CASE
filled_sections.text:
1. The Plaintiff and the Defendant first came into contact in the course of a business inquiry, and through discussions at {{PLACEHOLDER_NAME}} they agreed to enter into a formal business arrangement for which a written contract was subsequently prepared. The contract is evidenced by Annexure A and is dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}}. 
2. Pursuant to the terms of the Agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} (Annexure A), the Defendant undertook to provide the goods and/or services described in the agreement and the Plaintiff undertook to pay an advance of Rs. 2,000,000 to the Defendant, to be adjusted against the total consideration payable under the contract. The Agreement contemplated performance by the Defendant by a defined timeline and set out milestones and conditions for completion. 
3. In terms of the Agreement, the Plaintiff’s obligation was to make the advance payment on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} and the Defendant’s obligation was to perform and deliver the agreed goods/services by {{PERFORMANCE_DATE}} at the location specified in the contract. The contract also provided that non-performance would enable the Plaintiff to seek remedies as provided under the agreement and applicable law. 
4. On {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}}, the Plaintiff paid Rs. 2,000,000 to the Defendant as an advance toward the consideration due under the Agreement, using the payment mechanism evidenced by Annexure B. The payment was made to the Defendant in good faith in accordance with the terms of the contract, and a record of the transfer/receipt is contained in Annexure B. 
5. By {{DEFAULT_DATE}}, the Defendant had not performed the contracted obligations and there was no delivery or completion of the agreed goods/services by the performance deadline. The Plaintiff made multiple requests for performance and fulfillment of the obligations on dates that are recorded in the communications leading up to the breach, but the Defendant did not advance any substantive steps toward completion. As a result, the Plaintiff’s funds remained with the Defendant and the consideration under the Agreement was not met. 
6. After the default, the Plaintiff issued a legal notice dated {{NOTICE_DATE}} demanding repayment of the advance amount of Rs. 2,000,000 together with any lawful interest and costs, a copy of which is Annexure C. The Defendant’s reply to the legal notice was received on {{NOTICE_REPLY_DATE}} and is contained in Annexure D. In response to the ongoing non-performance, the Plaintiff filed the suit on {{FILING_DATE}}. The Plaintiff asserts that the facts constitute a failure of consideration and seeks recovery of the advance amount with interest and costs, supported by Annexures E and F as acknowledgment/admission and additional documents as needed.

filled_sections.heading: LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS
filled_sections.text:
Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872 creates a legal obligation of restoration where a contract is void, or where there is total failure of consideration, such that any person who has received any advantage under the contract is bound to restore it to the person from whom it was received. In the present case, the Plaintiff, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, on {{DATE_PAYMENT}} at {{PLACE}} paid {{DEFENDANT_NAME}} Rs. 2,000,000 as an advance for a business transaction described in Annexure A. This payment is evidenced by Annexure B, and the transaction, for reasons attributable to the Defendant’s default, failed on or about {{DATE_FAILURE}}, resulting in total failure of consideration as the Defendant did not confer any benefit or performance in return for the payment. By virtue of Section 65, the Defendant is thus obliged to restore the entire amount received, i.e., Rs. 2,000,000, to the Plaintiff, together with such interest as may be legally permissible and the costs of the suit, since the Plaintiff’s payment created a benefit that the Defendant has retained without just cause. The Defendant’s default is evidenced by the sequence of events reflected in Annexures A and B, and the Plaintiff has issued a legal notice, Annexure C, regarding the same; if any reply was received, Annexure D documents that response. The present pleading accordingly pleads total failure of consideration and seeks restitution of the amount advanced, with interest and costs, in accordance with the legal rule laid down in Section 65 and the supporting authorities cited in the verified provisions.

In the alternative and without prejudice to the above, Article 47 of the Limitation Act, described as the limitation provision applicable to money paid upon an existing consideration which afterwards fails, provides that such actions must be brought within three years from the accrual date, which is the date of failure. The factual matrix indicates that the accrual date is {{DATE_FAILURE}} and the present claim (including interest and costs) is pleaded within the permissible period as per Article 47, with the filing date to be specified as {{DATE_FILING}}. This alternative ground ensures that even if the court does not sustain the primary basis under Section 65, the claim remains actionable within the statutory window, given that there is no counter-claim or set-off raised by the Defendant and no forfeiture clause is relied upon or required for this cause of action. The Plaintiff reiterates that no part performance was rendered by the Defendant, and no adjustment or counter-claim is asserted by the Defendant; the relief sought remains restitution of Rs. 2,000,000 with interest and costs.

To the extent the Defendant contends that no part performance was rendered, that argument is immaterial to the obligations arising under Section 65 once a total failure of consideration is established, because the statutory remedy is restitution of the money received in circumstances of void or voidable contract or total failure of consideration. The Plaintiff relies on Annexure A for the underlying contract, Annexure B for the payment proof, and Annexure C together with Annexure D for the legal notice and any response, and reserves all rights with respect to any other documents that may become relevant. The relief sought is consistent with the governing legal framework, and the Plaintiff requests restitution in the sum of Rs. 2,000,000, with interest and costs, and such other relief as the Court may deem fit. Annexure E and Annexure F may contain any ancillary admissions or supporting documents as may be identified in these proceedings.

The territorial jurisdiction of this Court is proper in the District Court at {{COURT_PLACE}}, {{STATE}}, as the Defendant resides and carries on business at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}, which lies within the local limits of this Court's territorial jurisdiction. The cause of action first arose on {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}} in relation to the contract, and the Defendant defaulted on {{DEFAULT_DATE}} by failing to perform and to repay the sum due. This Court is invoked for pecuniary purposes because the claim amount is Rs 20,00,000, which falls within the monetary limits of the District Court for suits of this nature; the pecuniary limit for this Court is Rs {{PECUNIARY_LIMIT}}. By virtue of Section 65, the Defendant is thus obliged to restore the entire amount received, i.e., Rs. 2,000,000, to the Plaintiff, together with such interest as may be legally permissible and the costs of the suit, since the Plaintiff’s payment created a benefit that the Defendant has retained without just cause. blocking_issues.issue: Draft contains a stray drafting marker ("---SECTION_TEXT---") that is not pleading language and should not appear in a filed plaint.
blocking_issues.fix: Delete the marker entirely, or replace it with a proper placeholder in {{PLACEHOLDER}} format if some text is intended to be inserted there.
blocking_issues.location: After the "JURISDICTION" section, immediately before "FACTS OF THE CASE".

blocking_issues.issue: Facts section does not plead sufficient ‘material facts’ in a clear chronological narrative and relies heavily on vague references to the agreement/annexures (e.g., ‘goods and services described in the agreement’, ‘milestones and conditions’) without stating what the transaction was, what exactly was promised, and how/when it failed.
blocking_issues.fix: Rewrite the Facts section as a chronological story pleading material facts with specificity (use placeholders where details are not provided): nature of business transaction, key terms (total consideration, deliverables/obligations, timelines), advance payment particulars, plaintiff readiness, defendant’s specific defaults, communications/termination, and demand/refusal to refund showing total failure of consideration.

non_blocking_issues.issue: Prayer clause is presented as a single run-on paragraph; this is harder to read and may invite formatting objections.
non_blocking_issues.fix: Format each prayer as a separate indented sub-paragraph (a), (b), (c) etc., each on its own line.

non_blocking_issues.issue: Inconsistent interest placeholders/labels are used (e.g., {{PRE_SUIT_RATE}} vs {{PRE_SUIT_INTEREST_RATE}}; {{FUTURE_RATE}} vs {{FUTURE_INTEREST_RATE}}), which can lead to internal inconsistencies when filling values.
non_blocking_issues.fix: Use one consistent placeholder key per interest component across the Interest and Prayer sections.

non_blocking_issues.issue: List of documents numbering starts from 7 instead of 1.
non_blocking_issues.fix: Renumber the List of Documents from Serial No. 1 onwards.
